content.content:
title:	Remove ZFS broken-check override on classic-laddie once nixpkgs/zfs catches up to current zen kernel
state:	OPEN
author:	patflynn (Patrick Flynn)
labels:	
comments:	0
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
number:	532
--
## Background

PR #531 adds this temporary override in `hosts/classic-laddie/hardware.nix`:

```nix
# TEMPORARY UNBLOCK: ZFS 2.4.1 in current nixpkgs is marked broken against
# the linux-zen kernel we run (see modules/common/gaming.nix). Both
# `pkgs.zfs` (= zfs_2_4) and `pkgs.zfs_unstable` cap at
# kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor = "6.19", and our zen kernel is past that,
# so switching packages doesn't help — the broken check fires on both.
nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = "warn";
```

This was needed because the May 7 flake.lock bump pulled in a kernel newer than what nixpkgs's ZFS package officially supports. Without the override, the entire classic-laddie config refuses to evaluate (which broke main and PR #529's CI on 2026-05-07).

## What to track

- **Upstream issue**: https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/510485
- When nixpkgs ships a ZFS release whose `kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor` matches (or exceeds) the zen kernel we're running, this override becomes unnecessary.
- The check is mechanical: after a flake.lock update lands, evaluate classic-laddie *without* the override and confirm CI is green. If yes, drop the override.

## Acceptance

- The `nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = "warn"` line is removed from `hosts/classic-laddie/hardware.nix`.
- `build-nixos (classic-laddie)` CI job passes without it.
- The accompanying comment block is deleted (or pruned to a one-liner if any explanation is still useful).

## Why this matters

`broken = "warn"` is a structural hint that the combination *might* fail at runtime — e.g., the ZFS kmod could fail to load or have ABI issues against zen 7.0.3. Empirically the combo has worked, but every day with the override is a day where the next zen-kernel ABI change could surface as an unbootable system. The override should be load-bearing for as little time as possible.

## Suggested follow-up automation

Consider a klaus scheduled trigger that watches NixOS/nixpkgs#510485 (or the analogous future issue when ZFS bumps): when it closes / when ZFS lands a new release, klaus dispatches an agent to attempt removal and open a PR if CI goes green. Optional — manual triage works fine for something this rare.

content.text: The stable-kernel specialisation in `gaming.nix:55` already uses `pkgs.linuxPackages` as its fallback, so that's the proven-working stable. Let me apply the fix.

input.old_string:
boot.supportedFilesystems = [ "zfs" ];

  # TEMPORARY UNBLOCK: ZFS 2.4.1 in current nixpkgs is marked broken against
  # the linux-zen kernel we run (see modules/common/gaming.nix). Both
  # `pkgs.zfs` (= zfs_2_4) and `pkgs.zfs_unstable` cap at
  # kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor = "6.19", and our zen kernel is past that,
  # so switching packages doesn't help — the broken check fires on both.
  # Tracked upstream: https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/510485
  # Remove once nixpkgs ships a ZFS release that supports the running kernel.
  nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = "warn";
input.new_string:
boot.supportedFilesystems = [ "zfs" ];

  # ZFS upstream's kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor = "6.19"; the linux-zen
  # kernel pulled in by recent flake.lock updates (7.0.3 as of #528) is past
  # that, so ZFS 2.4.1 won't compile (configure-time "Cannot build against
  # kernel version 7.0.3-zen1"). Pin to stable until nixpkgs ships a ZFS
  # release with a higher kernel cap. PR #531 tried setting the broken flag
  # to "warn" instead — that silenced eval but didn't fix the actual build,
  # since CI dry-run doesn't compile kmods.
  #
  # sched-ext is upstream in 6.12+, so services.scx still works on stable.
  #
  # mkOverride 60 wins against the default-priority assignment in
  # modules/common/gaming.nix while still losing to the mkForce inside
  # the stable-kernel specialisation, preserving that fallback.
  boot.kernelPackages = lib.mkOverride 60 pkgs.linuxPackages;

content.content:
1	{
2	  config,
3	  lib,
4	  pkgs,
5	  ...
6	}:
7	
8	{
9	  imports = [
10	    ./hardware-configuration.nix
11	  ];
12	
13	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
14	  # Bootloader
15	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
16	  boot.loader.systemd-boot.enable = true;
17	  boot.loader.efi.canTouchEfiVariables = true;
18	
19	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
20	  # Networking
21	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
22	  networking.hostId = "8425e349"; # Required for ZFS
23	  networking.networkmanager.enable = true;
24	
25	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
26	  # Hardware - NVIDIA
27	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
28	  nixpkgs.config.allowUnfree = true;
29	  hardware.graphics.enable = true;
30	
31	  services.xserver.videoDrivers = [ "nvidia" ];
32	  hardware.nvidia = {
33	    modesetting.enable = true;
34	    powerManagement.enable = false;
35	    powerManagement.finegrained = false;
36	    open = false;
37	    nvidiaSettings = true;
38	    package = config.boot.kernelPackages.nvidiaPackages.stable;
39	  };
40	
41	  # Force-load NVIDIA display modules in initrd so the GPU drives the
42	  # framebuffer from boot. Without this, hardware.nvidia.modesetting.enable
43	  # wasn't enough on driver 595.58.03 + linux-zen 6.19.12 — the kernel
44	  # command line set nvidia-drm.modeset=1 but nothing actually loaded
45	  # nvidia_drm, leaving Hyprland stuck on EFI fb at 1024x768.
46	  boot.initrd.kernelModules = [
47	    "nvidia"
48	    "nvidia_modeset"
49	    "nvidia_drm"
50	  ];
51	
52	  # Allow qemu-libvirtd to access the GPU
53	  users.groups.video.members = [ "qemu-libvirtd" ];
54	  users.groups.render.members = [ "qemu-libvirtd" ];
55	
56	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
57	  # Storage Support
58	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
59	  boot.supportedFilesystems = [ "zfs" ];
60	
61	  # ZFS upstream's kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor = "6.19"; the linux-zen
62	  # kernel pulled in by recent flake.lock updates (7.0.3 as of #528) is past
63	  # that, so ZFS 2.4.1 won't compile (configure-time "Cannot build against
64	  # kernel version 7.0.3-zen1"). Pin to stable until nixpkgs ships a ZFS
65	  # release with a higher kernel cap. PR #531 tried setting the broken flag
66	  # to "warn" instead — that silenced eval but didn't fix the actual build,
67	  # since CI dry-run doesn't compile kmods.
68	  #
69	  # sched-ext is upstream in 6.12+, so services.scx still works on stable.
70	  #
71	  # mkOverride 60 wins against the default-priority assignment in
72	  # modules/common/gaming.nix while still losing to the mkForce inside
73	  # the stable-kernel specialisation, preserving that fallback.
74	  boot.kernelPackages = lib.mkOverride 60 pkgs.linuxPackages;
75	
76	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
77	  # USB stability
78	  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
79	  # Disable USB autosuspend to prevent enumeration timeouts during boot
80	  # (usb 6-3.1.1: device not accepting address, error -62) and Bluetooth
81	  # adapter power-management stalls that cause mouse stuttering.
82	  boot.kernelParams = [ "usbcore.autosuspend=-1" ];
83	
84	}
85

input.command:
git commit -am "$(cat <<'EOF'
classic-laddie: pin kernel to stable so ZFS builds

PR #531's `nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = "warn"` silenced
the eval-time broken check but the build still fails on a real rebuild:

    configure: error:
       *** Cannot build against kernel version 7.0.3-zen1.
       *** The maximum supported kernel version is 6.19.

CI dry-run doesn't compile kernel modules, so it never caught this.
classic-laddie boots from a ZFS root, so the kernel must have a working
ZFS module — the eval-time silencing wasn't a fix.

Pin classic-laddie's kernel to pkgs.linuxPackages (stable). mkOverride 60
wins against gaming.nix's default-priority assignment of linuxPackages_zen
while still losing to the mkForce inside the stable-kernel specialisation,
preserving that fallback path.

sched-ext was upstreamed in 6.12, so services.scx continues to work on
stable. Tracking issue #532 covers removing this pin once nixpkgs ZFS
catches up to current zen.
EOF
)"

input.command:
gh pr create --reviewer patflynn --title "classic-laddie: pin kernel to stable so ZFS builds" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

PR #531 added `nixpkgs.config.problems.handlers.zfs.broken = \"warn\"` to silence the ZFS broken check. CI passed because `nix build --dry-run` only evaluates — it doesn't compile kernel modules. On a real local rebuild today, the build fails:

```
configure: error:
   *** Cannot build against kernel version 7.0.3-zen1.
   *** The maximum supported kernel version is 6.19.
```

So the `broken` flag was actually correct — ZFS 2.4.1 literally cannot compile against the linux-zen 7.0.3 kernel pulled in by recent flake.lock updates (#528). The eval-time silence trick let evaluation succeed but the kmod build fails for real. classic-laddie boots from a ZFS root, so the kernel must have a working ZFS module.

## Why CI didn't catch this

The `build-nixos` job runs evaluation against the flake (effectively a dry-run path). Kernel module compilation only happens during a full system build / activation. So an eval-time-only silencing trick will always look green in CI even when the resulting system can't actually build.

This PR doesn't fix that gap — it just stops relying on eval-time-only validation for a build-time problem.

## Fix

Replace #531's eval-time workaround with an actual kernel pin in `hosts/classic-laddie/hardware.nix`:

```nix
boot.kernelPackages = lib.mkOverride 60 pkgs.linuxPackages;
```

Priority math:

- `modules/common/gaming.nix` assigns `boot.kernelPackages = pkgs.linuxPackages_zen` at default priority (100).
- The `stable-kernel` specialisation inside `gaming.nix` uses `lib.mkForce` (priority 50) to fall back to `pkgs.linuxPackages`.
- `lib.mkOverride 60` wins against the default-priority zen assignment but still loses to the `mkForce` inside the specialisation — the existing stable-kernel fallback continues to work as designed.

`pkgs.linuxPackages` is the proven-working stable target: it's the same attribute the `stable-kernel` specialisation already uses.

## sched-ext / services.scx

sched-ext was upstreamed in kernel 6.12, so the BPF scheduler used by `services.scx` continues to work on stable. No functional regression in gaming.nix's userspace.

## Tracking issue

Issue #532 was opened to track removal of #531's `broken=\"warn\"` override. After this PR lands, I'll comment there explaining the override was insufficient and either close it or repurpose it to track 'remove kernel pin once nixpkgs ZFS supports zen 7.x.'

## Verification

- CI eval will pass — but as noted above, that's not the gate that matters for this class of change.
- Real verification is a successful `nixos-rebuild switch` on classic-laddie.

Run: 20260509-1415-69f7
EOF
)"

content.text: Now let me leave the comment on issue #532 as required by the task.

input.command:
gh issue comment 532 --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Update: PR #531's `broken="warn"` override was insufficient. It silenced the eval-time check but the actual kmod build still fails on a real rebuild:

```
configure: error:
   *** Cannot build against kernel version 7.0.3-zen1.
   *** The maximum supported kernel version is 6.19.
```

CI dry-run doesn't compile kernel modules, so neither #531's CI nor this issue's "drop the override when CI is green" acceptance criterion would have caught the underlying problem. There was never a working state for the override to clean up — the override was always wrong-shaped.

PR #533 replaces #531 with an actual kernel pin (`boot.kernelPackages = lib.mkOverride 60 pkgs.linuxPackages` in `hosts/classic-laddie/hardware.nix`), removing the `broken="warn"` line entirely. sched-ext is upstream in 6.12+ so `services.scx` continues to work on stable.

Repurposing this issue: track removal of the kernel pin once nixpkgs ships a ZFS release whose `kernelMaxSupportedMajorMinor` matches or exceeds the running zen kernel. The mechanical check is the same shape as before — after a flake.lock bump lands, drop the pin and verify a real `nixos-rebuild switch` (not just CI eval) succeeds on classic-laddie.
EOF
)"

content.text:
PR opened: https://github.com/patflynn/cosmo/pull/533

The change pins classic-laddie's kernel to `pkgs.linuxPackages` via `lib.mkOverride 60`, removes #531's eval-time-only `broken="warn"` workaround, and added a comment on issue #532 explaining that the override was insufficient and repurposing the issue to track removal of the kernel pin once nixpkgs ZFS catches up to zen.
